prompt:
NetOps tooling ticket, Python. We run Meraki dashboards for several business units (one is in the Canadian region, one is heading to FedRAMP) and I'm constantly asked "what's offline right now?". I want a small stdlib-only package `merakiinv` that pulls a whole-estate inventory snapshot from the Cisco Meraki Dashboard API v1. test_meraki_inventory.py is the acceptance contract — `python3 test_meraki_inventory.py` runs everything against a loopback fake dashboard: no real Meraki org, no real API key, and the package itself must never sleep (waiting is injected).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Cisco Meraki Dashboard API v1 documentation — at minimum the Getting Started page (base URL plus the regional base URIs for Canada/China/India/US FedRAMP), the Rate Limit page (the 429 + Retry-After contract), the Pagination page (perPage/startingAfter and the RFC 5988 Link header), and the operation pages for listing organizations, an organization's networks, and per-organization device status. Careful with that last one: our old script called the organization device *statuses* endpoint and a teammate says it's deprecated now — confirm what the current docs say and build on the current replacement operation, and call the change out in your summary. Only first-party Cisco/Meraki documentation counts as a source. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json — both protected like the test file; if the live docs contradict something pinned there, report the conflict in your summary instead of editing protected files. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests import from `merakiinv`:
- `base_url_for_region(region)` for "global", "canada", "china", "india", "us-fedramp" → the exact versioned base URL from the docs; unknown region raises ValueError.
- `MerakiSession(api_key, base_url=<global default>, sleep=None, max_retries=3)` exposing `.base_url`; `get_json(path, params=None)`; `get_paginated(path, params=None, per_page=None)` returning the concatenated list across pages. Pagination must follow the Link header's next relation URL VERBATIM (both quoted and unquoted rel forms occur) without re-adding your own query parameters. List-valued params go on the wire in the dashboard's documented array convention. On a rate-limit response, wait exactly the server-mandated number of seconds via the injected sleep, retry at most max_retries times, then raise. All failures raise `MerakiAPIError` with `.status` and `.errors` decoded from the documented error envelope — and the API key must never appear in any error text.
- `collect_inventory(session, product_types=None, statuses=None, per_page=None)`: list all organizations (paginated), skip any org whose API access is disabled, then walk orgs in ascending id order — networks first, then device availability with the product/status filters applied — and return {"organizations": [{id, name, region, networks: [{id, name, productTypes (sorted)}] sorted by id, devices: [{serial, name, mac, productType, status, networkId}] sorted by serial}]}. region comes from the org's cloud region name.

Split the package across sensible modules — transport, pagination, traversal — at least 3 files plus `__init__.py`; your call on the exact split. test_meraki_inventory.py and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Meraki Dashboard API v1 REST (organizations, networks, device availabilities)

official_sources.title: Getting Started - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: primary base URL https://api.meraki.com/api/v1 and the regional base URIs api.meraki.ca, api.meraki.cn, api.meraki.in, api.gov-meraki.com (all with /api/v1); bearer authorization header format Authorization: Bearer <Meraki_API_Key>

official_sources.title: Rate Limit - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: 10 requests per second per organization with a burst of 30 in 2 seconds; HTTP 429 on exceeding the limit with a Retry-After header giving seconds to wait and body {"errors": ["API rate limit exceeded for organization"]}

official_sources.title: Pagination - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: perPage/startingAfter/endingBefore query parameters; RFC 5988 Link response header carrying up to first, prev, next, last relations whose URLs should be used directly rather than rebuilt

official_sources.title: Get Organizations - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations with perPage 3-9000; response objects carry id, name, url, api.enabled, licensing.model, cloud.region.name

official_sources.title: Get Organization Networks - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations/{organizationId}/networks with perPage 3-100000; response objects carry id, organizationId, name, productTypes, timeZone, tags

official_sources.title: Get Organization Devices Availabilities - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations/{organizationId}/devices/availabilities (the current replacement for the deprecated devices/statuses operation); perPage 3-1000 default 1000; productTypes/statuses/networkIds/serials/tags array filters; response objects {mac, name, network.id, productType, serial, status, tags} with status enum online, alerting, offline, dormant

research.language_rationale: This inventory job lives in a Python ops toolchain. The Dashboard API is documented as plain REST with bearer auth, Link-header pagination, and a JSON errors envelope, and Meraki's official Python library is a thin wrapper over exactly these endpoints; implementing against the documented REST contract with the stdlib keeps the acceptance tests hermetic and dependency-free while exercising the same wire semantics the official SDK relies on.
